observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:
(1) direct LDRMMO to ensure the timely submission of succeeding Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (LDRRMC) and Local Development Council (LDC) to the Audit Team; and moving forward
(2) direct MAO for timely preparation of Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF in accordance with the format as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated 12 September 2012, and
(3) strictly adhere on the provisions stipulated in the utilization of DRRMF.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
instruct MEO to:
(1.1) fast track the completion of delayed projects and copy furnish the Audit Team with Liquidating Damage (LD) report, if no further extensions will be given, and
(1.2) if opted for project termination, consider inclusion of the erring contractor or firm to the list of blacklisted contractors and submit the list to GPPB for dissemination; instruct BAC to
(2) conduct strict verification and validation whether the bidder has passed or not all the requirements and conditions as specified in the Bidding Documents1, and moving forward
(3) intensify monitoring and enforcing of implementation of eligible LDF PPAs in order to ensure that the LDF is optimally utilized, and
(4) adequately plan the development projects funded under the 20% and ensure that these are procurement-and-implementation-ready, and compliant with the guidelines under this JMC.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
(1) justify for the insufficiency of cash and cash equivalents to cover for the outstanding and valid obligations particularly under General Fund; and
(2) ensure that 5% Local Disaster Risk and Reduction Management Fund, 20% Local Development Fund and other Trust Funds be utilized for the purpose for which the funds were intended.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Exert diligent efforts to send demand letters and cause the collection of these long outstanding Accounts Receivables. For those found that collections were remote and could no longer be collectible, evaluate and provide an allowance on impairment and initiate the preparation of request for authority from the Commission on Audit to write-off the accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

reduce delays and discrepancies, thereby supporting accurate and complete
financial reporting, (2) reconcile the Reports of Collections and Deposits (RCDs)
and Cashbooks with the eTRACS system, through a control measure, such as
stamp “RCD reconciled with eTRACS” or certification, before forwarding them to
the Municipal Accounting Office (MAO) to ensure consistency and completeness
of collection data; MAO to (3) periodically reconcile the recorded collections in the
books of accounts with the collections reflected in the RCDs to identify and
address discrepancies promptly, and make necessary adjusting entries, if
warranted; and moving forward to (4) strengthen internal controls to ensure that
collections are recorded in the books of accounts upon receipt, in accordance
with the accrual basis of accounting under IPSAS 1.

subsequent costs to the Multi-Purpose Building, and if they correlate with the
existing asset and meet the criteria for capitalization, record them as additions or
improvements in accordance with applicable accounting and reporting standards.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct: MAO to (1) prepare and
maintain Notes to Financial Statements (NTFS) disaggregated by funding source,
in compliance with IPSAS 1, to ensure complete and transparent disclosures to
the financial statements, and (2) ensure that all items presented in the financial
statements are appropriately cross-referenced to corresponding explanatory
notes; and moving forward to (3) ensure compliance with presentation and
disclosure requirements prescribed under IPSAS and other applicable financial
reporting standards.

incurrence of the irregular expenditure in relation to the employer’s share in the

applicable guidelines and ensure that any employee contributions exceeding the
mandatory amount are supported by the concerned employee’s written consent;
(3) direct Municipal Accountant’s Office (MAO) to countercheck the payroll before
approval to ensure the prescribed rates and ceilings are correctly applied; and, (4)
moving forward apprise continuously with recent official issuances of HDMF to
ensure the timely adoption of the changes to the mandatory statutory requirements.

observe strictly the requirements of the law set forth in the abovementioned

related funds.

We recommended and the Management agreed to: (1) justify in writing the

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
a. Conduct a reconciliation of existing standalone improvements with the mother
assets to correct and avoid presentation of duplicate but similar buildings and
other structures; 
b. Conduct a comprehensive review of the useful life of buildings and other
structures, taking into account the improvements made and recompute the
depreciation expense in accordance with IPSAS 17 and IPSAS 3 to ensure that
the asset values and related depreciation charges reflect their updated service
potential and condition; 
c. Up-skill personnel involved in asset management and accounting through
targeted training sessions to enhance their understanding of IPSAS 17 and
NGAS guidelines, particularly accounting for PPE; and
d. Moving forward, develop and maintain a centralized tracking system or registry
for all buildings and other structures, including subsequent capitalizable costs
(e.g., improvements, major repairs), to ensure proper linkage, monitoring, and
timely capitalization under their respective mother assets.

correct discrepancies in asset values and accumulated depreciation; and
b. Update the depreciation schedule based on verified data and make the
necessary adjusting entries by debiting prior period adjustments and crediting
accumulated depreciation, incorporating adjustments related to subsequent
costs (e.g., improvements) previously treated as standalone assets into their
respective mother assets.

under Section 4 of RA No. 10023 via Special Patent, especially for those used

identified assets to reflect it in the financial statements in accordance with the
IPSAS 17 – Property, Plant and Equipment.
c. Moving forward, improve the individual documentation and recording process
regarding the depreciation of the property to prevent future errors.

        National  Government  since   they   prescribe   the   step-by-step
        procedures and documentary requirements to be complied by  LGUs  to
        allow a smooth implementation  of  the  project  and  avoid  future
        problems that might affect the continuity of project implementation
        such as land titling and/or right-of-way acquisition issues;


     3. Require the Municipal Accountant to:


        a)  Record in  the  Trust  Fund  books  all  ongoing  or  partially
           completed projects using the Construction  in  Progress  account
           and transfer  the  completed  projects  into  their  proper  PPE
           account for fair presentation in the financial statements. While
           the projects were not officially turned over yet by  the  source
           agencies  and  pending  compliance  of  any  lacking   documents
           relative to project completion and acceptance,  the  assets  may
           remain in the Trust Fund  books.  However,  require  the  source
           agency to have the projects officially turned over to  the  LGU,
           if warranted;


        b)    Observe the proposed accounting entries  illustrated  in  the
           IPSAS and GAM involving  fund  transfers  to  make  use  of  the
           correct accounts, prevent any  material  under/overstatement  of
           the PPE and Due to  NGAs  accounts  at  year-end,  and  properly
           present the actual nature of transactions; and


     4. Require the offices concerned to submit completely to the Municipal
        Accountant the necessary Disbursement Vouchers and  its  supporting
        documents under the Trust Fund such as  Inspection  and  Acceptance
        Reports and certificates of completion and/or acceptance to support
        the complete and timely recording of the assets and  other  related
        accounts in the trust fund books.

closely monitor and observe the  limitation  on  the  utilization  of  local
funds during the year to avoid exposure to overdraft and liquidity risk.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:

We recommended the following: The Municipal Treasurer shall send a written notice to the aforementioned payees for them to be aware of their outstanding checks. Notice to the payees shall be sent one month before the check becomes stale as required in Section 104 of GAM for LGUs. Stale checks in the hands of payees must be returned for cancellation and subject for replacement. If checks are returned by the payee, the Municipal Accountant shall request a certified copy of the DV from the Auditor for presentation to the Local Chief Executive who shall countersign the check. If the payees are untraceable or did not respond to the notice, the Municipal Accountant shall make the necessary adjusting entries.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Local Chief Executive to direct the Municipal Treasurer to: Strictly observe the prescribed frequency (once a week or reaching P10,000.00) of deposit of cash collections/receipts. If impracticable, at least 3 to 4 times a month. Assignment of staff to deposit the collections frequently could be considered. Practice deposit of all collections intactly and avoid retaining part of the collections as a cash reserve for cash disbursements. Set up a petty cash fund enough to cover petty disbursements. Any expenses not considered petty should be paid through check and not from collections. This is in compliance with Section 8 of COA Circular 92-382 and to safeguard public funds from any improper/illegal use.

We recommended the following:


     1. The HR personnel should seek the guidance  or  to  coordinate  with

        No. 24, series of 2017 (2017 ORAOHRA) to  establish  the  LGU’s  HR

        successfully formulate their own  HR  policies/hiring  plan/manual,
        especially in terms of manpower  requirement.  The  formulation  of
        these  policies  will  help  strengthen   the   internal   controls
        pertaining thereto. Also, the department heads and officials should
        be able to provide reasonable explanation on the need to hire  such
        number of employees under contracts and job orders.

        by the employees prior to the rendition of services  to  facilitate
        proper evaluation of work performed by these  employees.  Moreover,
        accomplishment  reports   should   be   evaluated   vis-a-vis   the
        terms/services agreed upon in the contract by HRMO personnel.

In  order  to  correct  the  aforementioned  deficiencies,   we
recommended the following measures:

   1. Management should stop or discourage  the  practice  of  resorting  to
      shopping as a mode of procurement particularly on reimbursement  basis
      for purchases of  goods,  supplies  and  materials  and  others.   The
      provisions of Republic Act No. 9184 and  its  Implementing  Rules  and
      Regulations (IRR) must be strictly adhered to with  considerations  on
      the relative factors as  right  quality,  quantity,  price,  time  and
      source at all times.


   2. Personnel responsible for  the  transactions  should  be  required  to
      explain in writing why the practice has been tolerated for  some  time
      despite the guidelines provided in the New Procurement Law.


   3. The BAC should be responsible in determining and recommending  to  the
      LCE the proper mode of procurement to be  done  in  every  procurement
      activity.


   4. Limit shopping  to  procurement  of  commonly  used  goods  except  on
      emergency cases only.

to:

   1. strictly enforce the settlement of the COA disallowances in compliance
      to Section 10.4 and 17.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated  September
      15, 2009 and COA Memorandum 2009-084 dated November 16, 2009. In  case
      of separated or deceased liable person, demand payments on  other  co-
      liable persons. If the deceased liable person happens to be without co-
      liable persons, submit to COA Audit Team the death certificate of  the
      said liable person to establish the actual death and shall  the  basis
      for the relief of accountability.


   2. require proper  documentation  and  issue  official  receipts  on  all
      payments of COA disallowances especially those who paid through salary
      deduction. Furnish the COA Audit Team and secure Notice of  Settlement
      to reflect the payments on COA records.

concerned to:

        implementation of GAD-related programs and  projects  in  order  to
        attain the objective for which funds were provided;

     3. direct the GAD Focal Point System and other personnel concerned  to
        strictly adhere to the  provisions  of  JMC  No.  2013-01  so  that
        formulation, costing and  allocation,  submission,  implementation,
        monitoring,  evaluation  and   accounting   of   results   of   the
        Municipality’s annual GPB and GAD Accomplishment Reports will be in

        will be responsible in supervising, implementing and monitoring the

        performance  of   its   functions   and   the   accomplishment   of
        targets/projects embodied in the approved  10-year  ESWM  Plan  and
        other matters concerning the office.

observations.recommendation:
Management should strictly adhere to the provisions of Republic Act 9184 on its procurement activities. specifically, we    recommend the following:

 Adopt competitive public bidding as the general mode of procurement in accordance with Section 8 and 48.2 of the IRR-A of Republic Act 9184.

Limit the use of alternative modes of procurement only under highly exceptional cases as provided for in Sections 49-54 of  the IRR-A of R.A. 9184.

Require each department to prepare their respective Annual Procurement Plan (APP) and to update the same regularly every six months or as often as the need arises for proper consolidation by the BAC Secretariat.

 Require each department to meticulously and judiciously plan their procurement and require that no procurement shall be undertaken unless in accordance with the Annual Procurement Plan.

 Require the consolidation of the procurement needs of all departments and integrate into one Annual Procurement Plan for the Municipality of Sarangani as a whole and which will be the basis of the bidding activities in bulk and not individually by departments to save on time and costs.

No splitting of    requisitions /purchase orders.

g) Avail of the services of the G-EPS and the Procurement Service of the Department of Budget and Management for its procure-ment needs to avail of much lower prices to the best advantage of the government.

h)Strictly   require    the submission by all bidders of the Affidavit of Disclosure of Relations as required under  Section 47 of the IRR-A of RA 9184.

i) The procuring entity, as a general rule, shall adopt public bidding as the general mode of procurement and shall see to it that the procurement program allows sufficient lead time for such public bidding. Alternative methods shall be resorted to only in highly exceptional cases provided for in the implementing rules and regulations of RA 9184. The requirements prescribed in Sections 51 and 52 of RA 9184 shall be strictly observed in the procurement of goods thru repeat order and shopping, respectively.
observations.managementAction: Proper mode of procurement has been observed.

observations.recommendation: 1. Formally create the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Office (MENRO) and employ/appoint head of this Office that will be responsible in supervising, implementing and monitoring the 10-year Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan of the municipality. 2. Allocate reasonable budget for the newly created office so as to perform its functions and accomplish targets/projects embodied in the approved 10-year ESWM Plan and other matters concerning the office. 3. Employ additional manpower in the Eco-Park and Waste Management Project and in solid waste collection services to strengthen its operation efficiently and effectively. 4. Strengthen efforts in maintaining accessible roads and fast-track any on-going rehabilitation on of roads as it is essential for the garbage trucks to collect solid waste to remote barangays. 5. Intensify dissemination and educational campaign to enhance awareness on proper waste disposal. 6. Strictly implement segregation of solid wastes and establishment of “functional” material recovery facility on all barangays of the municipality. 7. Review the 10-year ESWM Plan at least every 2 years or as the need arises as mandated by RA 9003, so as to assess the workability/viability of the targets/plans in relationship to actual developments. Come up with the updated 10-year ESWM Plan should there be significant changes or developments of the plan.

observations.recommendation:
We  recommended  that  the  Local  Chief  Executive  will  direct  the
designated General Services Officer,  in  coordination  with  the  Municipal
Accountant, to:

 a. Completely maintain and update the Stock Cards and Property Cards;


 b. Conduct the annual  physical  count  of  all  the  property,  plant  and
    equipment owned by the  Municipality  to  establish  the  existence  and
    correct the balances of PPE accounts;

 c. Prepare and submit promptly the Report on the Physical Count of PPE  not
    later than January 31 of each year; and

 d. If warranted, consider  the  official  creation  the  Municipal  General
    Services Office and appoint a fulltime GSO to spearhead the said office.
    Also, allocate reasonable budget for its operation.

Accountant  to  strictly  observe   the   above-mentioned   guidelines.   In
connection   to   this,   we   further   recommended   the   transfer    and
reclassification of accounts as follows:

      A subsidiary account by year shall be maintained to ensure  the  clear
monitoring of the funds and that after the  5-year  period,  any  unutilized
amount shall be reverted back to the unappropriated surplus of  the  General
Fund and shall be available for use  in  the  disaster  risk  reduction  and
management activities and other social services after  subsequent  enactment
by the Local Sanggunian.

We recommended the Management to:


   1. Require the Municipal Accountant to regularly  prepare  on  a  monthly
      basis the Report on the Sources and Utilization of  LDRRMF  using  the
      prescribed format in COA Circular No.  2012-002  dated  September  12,
      2012; and


   2. Submit such report on or before the 15th day  after  the  end  of  the
      month through the LDRRMC and the Local Development Council to the  COA
      Auditor in compliance with the reportorial requirements of the  above-
      mentioned COA Circular.

We   recommended   the   Local   Government   to   conduct    proper
planning/meeting on the implementation of all  programmed  projects  in  the
70% of calamity fund, especially under capital outlay projects. This  is  to
make sure that  the  LGU  has  a  proper  direction  in  the  implementation
process.

      We further recommended the LDRRM Officer, Municipal Engineer and  MPDO
to fast-track and closely  monitor  these  projects.  Should  there  be  any
hindrances/problems/delays noted during implementation,  it  should  be  the
initiative of the concerned personnel to conduct a meeting  or  do  whatever
necessary means to address/resolve such.

We recommended the Local  Chief  Executive  to  direct  the  Municipal
Treasurer to strictly observe the COA-MOF Joint Cir.  No.  1-81  prescribing
the frequency of deposit of cash collections/receipts.

      Moreover, we specifically recommended to practice depositing  of  “all
collections in local treasury” as soon as it reaches P2,000.00 or  at  least
weekly whenever impracticable.  This  is  to  implement/observe  sound  cash
management and to safeguard public funds  from  possible  loss  or  improper
use.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant and Municipal  Budget  Officer
to  reconcile  their  reports  and  determine  the  actual  that  should  be
available for incomplete/unimplemented 20% development projects.

      We  further  recommended  the  management,  upon  authorization   from
Sanggunian Bayan, to open a separate bank account in the  General  Fund  for
the  20%  Development  Fund.  Upon  opening,  the  Municipal  Treasurer  and
Accountant should be directed to transfer the determined/reconciled  balance
of the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended the Management to:


     1. Require the Local Development Council to come up  with  a  detailed
        Annual Investment Plan (AIP) through consultation and  deliberation
        with the concerned Municipal and Barangay officials;


     2. Incorporate in the AIP the complete  description  of  the  project,
        exact location, intended beneficiaries and estimated cost  of  each
        project to promote transparency and accountability; and

      3.  Make sure that only those projects in the  approved  AIP  will  be
        implemented. Otherwise, the Sanggunian should pass a resolution for
        the reversion, if warranted.

We recommended the Local  Chief  Executive  to  direct  the  Municipal
Planning and Development Officer to review thoroughly  the  cited  programs
and projects for the P14,449,104.12 remaining fund and give priority to the
most needed projects/programs of the residents.


      Conduct adequate planning so that programmed projects  in  the  Annual
Investment Plan (AIP) be reasonably attained in  the  current  year  and  to
obtain optimum utilization of the 20% Development Fund.

      Finally, we recommended to  ensure  that  unexpended  balance  of  the
current  year  be  reprogrammed/re-appropriated  in  the  ensuing  year   as
prescribed in Section 322 of RA 7160.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to direct the  concerned  LGU
personnel/focal persons to:

   1. Re-assess the labor force requirement in the implementation of DA  and
      BFAR funded projects and add a reasonable manpower;


   2. Conduct strategical planning in  all  implementation  of  projects  in
      order to finish the projects as  soon  as  possible.  This  will  also
      address the other problems of implementing personnel as  far  as  they
      are concerned; and

   3. Fast track the completion of  the  above-mentioned  BUB  projects  and
      regularly monitor its status of implementation. Also, set  the  target
      date   of   completion   in   all   projects    as    a    basis    of
      evaluation/monitoring.

We recommended that the Local Chief  Executive  direct  the  Municipal
Treasurer to remit the provincial share of  the  SEF  collection  within  15
days of every succeeding month. The Municipal Treasurer should also  revisit
R.A. 5447 to familiarize his duties and responsibilities on the handling  of
SEF collections.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor thru the Municipal Treasurer  to
facilitate   the   re-evaluation   and   updating    of    the    collection
schemes/strategies employed by the Municipality and to extend  extra  effort
to increase collection effectiveness and efficiency of both the current  and
delinquent receivables.

      Also, we  recommended  to  the  Municipal  Treasurer  to  revisit  the
provisions in the RA No. 7160  regarding  the  collection  of  RPT  and  SET
Receivables to enable him to acquire more confidence from  the  Act  and  to
implement effectively the collection of these receivables.

We recommended the management to remit the collection held in trust to
 the BIR within  the  prescribed  period  to  avoid  additional  charges  or
 penalties, interest and possible misuse of funds.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to instruct the  Municipal
Treasurer to remit all the unremitted mandatory obligations, such  as  GSIS,
Pag-Ibig and Phil-health  promptly  to  avoid  unnecessary  penalty  charges
enforced by the concerned  depository  agencies  to  the  employees.  It  is
stressed  that  penalty/interest  incurred  due  to  delayed   and/or   non-
remittance shall be a personal liability of the Municipal Accountant.

We recommended the following:


   1. The Municipality formulate hiring plan of casuals,  contractuals  /job
      orders to strengthen internal controls pertaining thereto.  Department
      heads and officials should be able to provide  reasonable  explanation
      on the need to hire  such  number  of  employees  under  contracts/job
      orders;

   2. The functions and duties of the  contracted  employees  must  be  well
      defined in their contracts  and  are  clear,  known  and  accepted  by
      employees  prior  to  rendition  of  services  to  facilitate   proper
      evaluation of work performed by these employees.

Accomplishment reports should be evaluated  vis-a-vis  the  terms/  services
agreed upon in the contract by personnel of HRMO; and

   3. Employees who have been working with the Municipality for more than 10
      years may be hired as  a  casual  employee  charged  under  PS  budget
      especially those who are performing essential and  necessary  services
      of a regular staff considering the  ability  of  the  Municipality  to
      appropriate items under PS budget without exceeding the prescribed  PS
      limitation.

The Audit Team recommended to:

   1. Formally create the Municipal Environment and Natural Resources Office
      (MENRO)  and  employ/appoint  head  of  this  Office  that   will   be
      responsible in supervising, implementing and  monitoring  the  10-year
      Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan of the municipality; and

   2. Allocate reasonable budget for the  newly  created  office  so  as  to
      perform its functions and accomplish targets/projects embodied in  the
      approved 10-year ESWM Plan and other matters concerning the office.

We recommended the Local Government to:

   1.  Review  the  budget  appropriations  for  Solid   Waste   Management
      Programs/Projects and appropriate reasonable budget enough to realize
      the targets set in 10-year ESWM Plan of the Municipality;


   2. Expedite the implementation of ongoing projects and employ additional
      manpower that is necessary for its efficient implementation; and


   3. Prioritize the implementation of the targets in the  ESWM  plan  that
      were not yet started. Also, conduct monitoring and review on the ESWM
      10-year plan and assess  whether  there  is  a  need  to  revise  the
      existing plan.

We recommended the following:


   1.  Establish GAD  Database  to  serve  as  basis  for  gender-responsive
      planning, programming and policy formulation.  The  GAD  database  can
      either be manually operated or developed through  software  and  shall
      form part of the overall Management Information System  (MIS)  of  the
      agency; and

   2. Reassess the manpower requirement in the implementation  of  GAD  PPAs
      and add additional fulltime  GAD  Officer  that  will  constitute  the
      Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) Team to expedite the implementation of
      unutilized balance of GAD Budget.

We  recommended  that  the  Municipality  should  strictly  adhere  to
Republic Act 9344 or  known  as  Juvenile  Justice  Act  of  2006  and  DILG
Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 as stated above  by  utilizing  the  1%  of
Internal  Revenue  Allotment  (IRA)  to  establish  the  Local  Council  for
Protection of Children (LCPC).  We  further  recommend  that  the  Municipal
Planning and Development Officer  (MPDO)  and  Municipal  Social  Welfare  &
Development Officer (MSWD) or whoever  official  assigned  as  focal  person
will  take  the  initiative  to  coordinate  with  the  Municipal  Mayor  in
establishing Local Councils for the Protection of Children (LCPC).

We recommended the Local Chief Executive through Municipal  Accountant
to strictly  enforce  the  settlement  of  the  disallowances  amounting  to
P583,173.85 in compliance to Section 10.4 and 17.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-
006 dated September 15, 2009 and COA Memorandum 2009-084 dated November  16,
2009.


      Furthermore, we recommended to require those persons  liable  who  are
still in the service to give preferential attention in settling their  audit
disallowances.  This  will  avoid  the  management  to  have  difficulty  in
collecting their amounts due  whenever  they  will  be  separated  from  the
service by any means.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendations:

      We recommend that the Local  Chief  Executive  direct  the  designated
General Services Officer, in coordination  with  the  Municipal  Accountant,
to:

 a. Conduct the annual  physical  count  of  all  the  property,  plant  and
    equipment owned by the  Municipality  to  establish  the  existence  and
    correct the balances of PPE accounts;
 b. Prepare promptly the Report on the Physical Count of PPE;
 c. Submit religiously the RPCPPE to the Auditor concerned  not  later  than
    January 31 of each year; and
 d. Require the Appraisal Team to prepare Appraisal Report and submit it  to
    the Commission on Audit for Technical Inspection.

      We also recommend that the GSO-Designate and the Municipal  Accountant
shall  completely  maintain  the  prescribed  Property   Cards   and   PPELC
respectively and reconcile it with  the  RPCPPE  to  establish  the  correct
amount of the PPE in the financial statements.

      Finally, we recommend  to  officially  create  the  Municipal  General
Services Office and appoint a fulltime GSO to  spearhead  the  said  office.
Also, allocate reasonable budget for its operation.

Recommendations:

Recommendations:

Observations. Moreover, the Municipal Agriculturist  commented  that  he  is

to expedite the projects funded by BUB program and to address  the  problems
encountered so as not to hamper its implementation.

Annual Investment Plan (AIP) can be reasonably attained in the current  year
and to obtain its optimum utilization.

those of the prior years  be  reprogrammed/re-appropriated  in  the  ensuing
year as prescribed in Section 4 of DILG MC No. 97-30.

shortcomings and expressed their will to really plan well all  the  projects
and fast track all ongoing development projects in 20% Development Fund.

concerned personnel to see to it that projects in 20% Development  Fund  are
well planned and implemented.

Planning and Development Coordinator,  to  specify  clearly  in  the  Annual

and reliable information as to its assets, liabilities and equity.

(Statement of Financial Position, Statement  of  Financial  Performance  and
Statement of Cash Flows) should be prepared and submitted regularly  to  COA
as provided by above-stated guidelines.

every school in the  locality,  include  in  the  plan/budget  the  suitable

priority  to  the  (i)  construction,  repair  and  maintenance  of   school
buildings and other facilities of public elementary and  secondary  schools;
(ii) establishment and maintenance of extension  classes,  where  necessary;
(iii) sports activities at the division, district,  municipal  and  barangay
levels  and  seriously  optimize  the  utilization  of  it   so   that   the
student/pupils/stakeholders will get the benefits  that  were  intended  for
them.

Recommendations:

      We recommend the following:

1. Formally create the Municipal Environment and  Natural  Resources  Office
(MENRO) and employ/appoint head of this Office that will be  responsible  in
supervising, implementing and monitoring the 10-year Ecological Solid  Waste
Management Plan of the municipality.

2. Allocate reasonable budget for the newly created office so as to  perform
its functions and accomplish targets/projects embodied in the  approved  10-
year ESWM Plan and other matters concerning the office.

3. Employ additional manpower in the Eco-Park and Waste  Management  Project
and  in  solid  waste  collection  services  to  strengthen  its   operation
efficiently and effectively.

4. Strengthen efforts in maintaining accessible roads and fast-track any on-
going rehabilitation of roads as it is essential for the garbage  trucks  to
collect solid waste to remote barangays.

5. Intensify dissemination and educational campaign to enhance awareness  on
proper waste disposal.

6. Strictly implement segregation  of  solid  wastes  and  establishment  of
“functional”  material  recovery  facility   on   all   barangays   of   the
municipality.

7. Review the 10-year ESWM Plan at least  every  2  years  or  as  the  need
arises as mandated by RA 9003, so as to assess the workability/viability  of
the targets/plans in relationship to actual developments. Come up  with  the
updated  10-year  ESWM  Plan  should  there  be   significant   changes   or
developments of the plan.

observations.recommendation: Stop granting cash advances without specific purpose. Any transfer or reversion of accounts from petty cash fund to payroll fund should be duly supported with complete documentatio ns to validate the necessity of such transfers. Identify the need for maintaining petty cash fund, require accountable officers to return the existing funds if found unnecessary. Stop granting another cash advances to officials and employees unless the previous ones are fully liquidated. Otherwise, failure of the accountable officer to liquidate his cash advance within the prescribed period shall constitute a valid cause for the withholding of his salary and institution of other sanctions as provided for under Sections 9.2 and 9.3 of the Circular. In this regard, we recommend that subsidiary ledgers (SL) which contain the details or breakdown of the balances of the controlling account appearing in the General Ledger should be completely provided with complete information such as the date when the cash advance was granted and liquidated, the particulars and/or purpose or details pertaining to the transaction and the journal entry or disbursement voucher number in the reference column. Likewise, the SL should be currently updated for it is the basis or record for the accountant to certify if an accountable officer can apply for another cash advance or not. Require the Accountant to record all liquidations for travel in the general journal and not in the cash disbursemen ts journal.

Direct the Municipal Accountant to send demand letters for the immediate settlement and or refund of all prior years’ unliquidated cash advances. Advise also the accountable officers concerned whose cash advances were for special purpose to settle and make a proper accounting as soon as the purpose has already been served. Adhere strictly to the provision in COA Circular No. 97-002 particularly Section 4.2.1.

observations.recommendation: Management should strictly adhere to the provisions of Republic Act 9184 on its procurement activities. Specifically we recommend the following: Adopt competitive public bidding as the general mode of procurement in accordance with Section 8 and 48.2 of the IRR-A of Republic Act 9184. Limit the use of alternative modes of procurement only under highly exceptional cases as provided for in Sections 49-54 of the IRR-A of R.A. 9184. Require each department to prepare their respective Annual Procurement Plan (APP) and to update the same regularly every six months or as often as the need arises for proper consolidation by the BAC Secretariat. Require each department to meticulously and judiciously plan their procurement and require that no procurement shall be undertaken unless in accordance with the Annual Procurement Plan. Require the consolidation of the procurement needs of all departments and integrate into one Annual Procurement Plan for the Municipality of Sarangani as a whole and which will be the basis of the bidding activities in bulk and not individually by departments to save on time and costs. No splitting of requisitions /purchase orders. Avail of the services of the G-EPS and the Procurement Service of the Department of Budget and Management for its procurement needs to avail of much lower prices to the best advantage of the government. Strictly require the submission by all bidders of the Affidavit of Disclosure of Relations as required under Section 47 of the IRR-A of RA 9184. The procuring entity, as a general rule, shall adopt public bidding as the general mode of procurement and shall see to it that the procurement program allows sufficient lead time for such public bidding. Alternative methods shall be resorted to only in highly exceptional cases provided for in the implementing rules and regulations of RA 9184. The requirements prescribed in Sections 51 and 52 of RA 9184 shall be strictly observed in the procurement of goods thru repeat order and shopping, respectively.
